draft.introduction: The Divan of Hafiz of Shiraz (c. 1370 CE) is the most beloved collection of lyric poetry in Persian literature. His ghazals celebrate wine, love, and the beloved while operating simultaneously as Sufi spiritual allegory. Goethe called him his “twin.”

translation.text: O cupbearer, let the wine flow round and hand the cup along, for love seemed easy at first, but then grew difficult. The musk-scent that the morning breeze will spread from her unfolded curls—what blood dripped from the hearts of lovers, twisted like those curls! Stain your prayer carpet with wine if the master commands, for the traveler knows well the customs of every stage along the way.
translation.original_text: الا یا ایها الساقی ادر کاسا و ناولها

translator_notes.note: Hafiz’s poetry deliberately sustains multiple levels of meaning: the wine is both literal and the intoxication of divine love; the beloved is both human and divine; the cupbearer is both a tavern servant and the spiritual master. This polysemy is intentional—it reflects the Sufi principle that the sensible and spiritual worlds mirror each other, and that beauty at any level points toward the ultimate Beauty.
